subject: mathematics

sections.title: Thinking about 'Think of a Number' Tricks

prose_summary:
Section 6.1 introduces algebra as a playful instrument, framing the chapter as an investigation of tricks and puzzles and explaining how algebra can both decode and invent such games. The section sets the tone by recalling that students have already used algebra to model situations and solve equations, and now invites them to apply those tools recreationally.

Section 6.2 explains and applies algebraic reasoning to 'Think of a Number' tricks. The section opens with a familiar five-step trick that always yields 2, then shows step-by-step how substituting a variable x for the unknown number reduces the trick to a constant expression (x + 2 − x = 2). It then extends this to a two-variable date-guessing trick involving month M and day D, where a sequence of multiplications and additions encodes both values into a single number (100M + 165 + D). The student decodes the result by subtracting 165 and reading off the month (hundreds digit) and day (last two digits). The section includes student practice in decoding dates from given final answers, and Math Talk prompts to modify tricks or invent entirely new ones. The dominant act is reasoning: explaining why a constant-outcome trick works algebraically and deriving the decoding formula.

Section 6.3 defines and explores number pyramids, where each cell equals the sum of the two cells directly below it. The section first applies the pyramid rule forward (given the base, fill upward) and then solves backward (given the top and some base entries, use equations to find the missing value). A three-cell base pyramid with top value 60, left base 12, right base 8, and an unknown middle entry is solved by setting up three equations and solving for the unknown (c = 20). The section then generalises: for a three-row pyramid with bottom row (a, b, c), the top is a + 2b + c. The Figure it Out problems ask students to apply and extend this formula to larger pyramids and to connect pyramid coefficients with the Virahāṅka-Fibonacci sequence, requiring them to reason about patterns without constructing the full pyramid. The dominant act is reasoning: deriving algebraic expressions for pyramid tops and justifying numerical patterns.

Section 6.4 explores two grid-based algebraic puzzles. The first is Calendar Magic: a 2×2 grid of calendar dates has entries a, a+1, a+7, a+8, so the sum is 4a + 16. Given a sum, solving for a recovers all four dates. A Math Talk prompt invites students to design grids of other shapes and sizes. The second subsection, Algebra Grids, uses shape-symbol equations where each row sums to a given value; students solve the system to find the value of each shape. Both puzzles demonstrate that algebra converts visual or numerical pattern-recognition into solvable equations. The dominant act is applying algebra to solve simultaneous equations embedded in visual grids.

Section 6.5 investigates how to maximise the product formed by arranging three given digits into a two-digit multiplicand and a one-digit multiplier. The section first works through digits 2, 3, 5 by exhaustive comparison of six candidates, grouping them by multiplier, eliminating inferior options, and using algebraic expansion to compare the two finalists (32 × 5 vs 52 × 3). It then proves the general rule: for three digits p < q < r, the largest product is always qp × r — largest digit as multiplier, remaining two digits in descending order. The proof compares expanded forms and shows the critical comparison reduces to a single term. The dominant act is reasoning: proving a general optimisation rule using algebraic expansion.

Section 6.6 applies algebra to divisibility tricks. The lead trick: choose a 2-digit number ab, reverse it to ba, find the difference. Using place-value notation (10b + a) − (10a + b) = 9(b − a), the section proves the difference is always divisible by 9. The Figure it Out problems extend this reasoning to sums of reversed digits (divisibility by 11), cyclic permutations of 3-digit numbers (divisibility by 37 and 3), the abcabc pattern (divisibility by 7, 11, 13), and a series of word problems requiring students to set up and solve linear equations (shrines and flowers, horses and hens, age problems, cowherds, dosa cart economics, fraction sequences). The final problem, 'Karim and the Genie,' is a rich multi-part puzzle where students reverse-engineer a doubling-and-payment scheme to find the initial coin count and analyse the genie's strategy algebraically. The section concludes with the chapter summary. The dominant acts are reasoning (proving divisibility properties algebraically) then applying (solving linear equations in word problems).

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out, section 6.2 p.136
enumerated_exercises.description: How would you change this game to make the final answer 3? What about 5?

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Math Talk, section 6.2 p.136
enumerated_exercises.description: Can you come up with more complicated steps that always lead to the same final value?

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out, section 6.2 p.137
enumerated_exercises.description: Find the dates if the final answers are the following: (i) 1269 (ii) 394 (iii) 296 — decode each result using the date-encoding trick.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Math Talk, section 6.2 p.137
enumerated_exercises.description: Can you change the steps in this trick and still find the original date? Instead of subtracting 165 from the final answer, you might have to subtract some other number.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Math Talk, section 6.2 p.137
enumerated_exercises.description: Try to devise your own 'Think of a Number' trick.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out, section 6.3 p.138
enumerated_exercises.description: Use the same rule to fill these pyramids: (a) base row 6, 2; (b) base row 3, 4, 3; (c) base row 5, 4, 5, 0 — fill all missing cells upward.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out, section 6.3 p.138
enumerated_exercises.description: What about filling in the numbers in this pyramid? Where do we start? Top is 60, bottom-left is 12, bottom-right is 8, and the middle bottom cell is unknown.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out, section 6.3 p.139
enumerated_exercises.description: Fill the following pyramids: (a) top 50, one entry 22, base entries include 4 and 6; (b) one entry 40, base entries include 5, 7, and 9; (c) top 35, base entries include 3, 5, and 7.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out, section 6.3 p.139
enumerated_exercises.description: What is the relationship between the numbers in the bottom row and the number at the top? Investigate for pyramids of two and three rows using letter-numbers.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q1, section 6.3 p.140
enumerated_exercises.description: Without building the entire pyramid, find the number in the topmost row given the bottom row in each case: (a) 4, 13, 8; (b) 7, 11, 3; (c) 10, 14, 25.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q2, section 6.3 p.140
enumerated_exercises.description: Write an expression for the topmost row of a pyramid with 4 rows in terms of the values in the bottom row.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q3, section 6.3 p.140
enumerated_exercises.description: Without building the entire pyramid, find the number in the topmost row given the bottom row in each case: (a) 8, 19, 21, 13; (b) 7, 18, 19, 6; (c) 9, 7, 5, 11.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q4, section 6.3 p.140
enumerated_exercises.description: If the first three Virahāṅka-Fibonacci numbers are written in the bottom row of a three-row number pyramid, fill in the rest. What numbers appear? Is the top a Virahāṅka-Fibonacci number?

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q5, section 6.3 p.140
enumerated_exercises.description: What can you say about the numbers in the pyramid and the number at the top when: (i) first four Virahāṅka-Fibonacci numbers in the bottom row of a four-row pyramid; (ii) first 29 Virahāṅka-Fibonacci numbers in the bottom row of a 29-row pyramid?

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q6, section 6.3 p.140
enumerated_exercises.description: If the bottom row of an n-row pyramid contains the first n Virahāṅka-Fibonacci numbers, what can we say about the numbers in the pyramid and the number at the top?

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out, section 6.4 p.141
enumerated_exercises.description: Suppose you are told that the sum of a 2×2 calendar grid is 36. Can you find the 4 numbers in the grid?

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Math Talk, section 6.4 p.142
enumerated_exercises.description: Create your own calendar trick. Choose a grid of a different size and shape.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out, section 6.4 p.142
enumerated_exercises.description: In the following grids, find the values of the shapes and fill in the empty squares: (a) 3×3 grid with squares and circles, row sums 27 and 21; (b) 3×3 grid with circles and diamonds, row sums 18 and 15.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q1, section 6.5 p.144
enumerated_exercises.description: Fill the digits 1, 3, and 7 in □□ × □ to make the largest product possible.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q2, section 6.5 p.144
enumerated_exercises.description: Fill the digits 3, 5, and 9 in □□ × □ to make the largest product possible.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out, section 6.6 p.145
enumerated_exercises.description: Can you work out what happens if a > b? (In the digit-reversal difference trick, when the original number is larger than its reverse.)

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q1, section 6.6 p.145
enumerated_exercises.description: In the digit-reversal trick, what is the quotient when you divide by 9? Is there a relationship between the two numbers and the quotient?

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q2, section 6.6 p.145
enumerated_exercises.description: Instead of finding the difference of the two 2-digit numbers and their reverse, find their sum. Observe examples (31+13=44, 28+82=110, 12+21=33). Is the sum always divisible by 11? Justify using algebra.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q3, section 6.6 p.145
enumerated_exercises.description: Consider any 3-digit number abc (100a + 10b + c). Form bca and cab by cycling digits. Add all three. Justify using algebra that the sum is always divisible by 37. Is it also divisible by 3?

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q4, section 6.6 p.146
enumerated_exercises.description: Consider any 3-digit number abc. Make a 6-digit number abcabc. Divide by 7, then by 11, then by 13. What do you get? Try other numbers. Figure out why it works. [Hint: Multiply 7, 11, and 13.]

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q5, section 6.6 p.146
enumerated_exercises.description: There are 3 shrines each with a magical pond that doubles flowers. A person dips, places flowers at each shrine in equal numbers. How many flowers did he start with? How many flowers did he place in each shrine?

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q6, section 6.6 p.146
enumerated_exercises.description: A farm has horses and hens. Total heads = 55, total legs = 150. How many horses and how many hens? Can you solve without letter-numbers?

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q7, section 6.6 p.146
enumerated_exercises.description: A mother is 5 times her daughter's age. In 6 years' time, the mother will be 3 times her daughter's age. How old is the daughter now?

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q8, section 6.6 p.146
enumerated_exercises.description: Gauri says to Naina: 'You have twice as many cows as I do.' Naina says: 'If I gave you three of my cows, we would each have the same number.' How many cows do each have?

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q9, section 6.6 p.146
enumerated_exercises.description: Dosa cart problem: rent ₹5000/day, cost ₹10/dosa. (i) If 100 dosas sold, what selling price gives ₹2000 profit? (ii) If selling price is ₹50, how many dosas for ₹2000 profit?

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q10, section 6.6 p.146
enumerated_exercises.description: Evaluate the sequence of fractions: 1/3, (1+3)/(5+7), (1+3+5)/(7+9+11). What do you observe? Explain why this happens. [Hint: Sum of first n odd numbers.]

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q11, section 6.6 p.147
enumerated_exercises.description: Karim and the Genie: coins double each tree round, 8 coins paid each round, after 3 rounds Karim is left with exactly 8 coins. (i) How many coins did Karim start with? (ii) For what cost per round should Karim agree to increase his coins? (iii) How should the genie set the cost to get all of Karim's coins?
